name: Enzyme — Automatic Differentiation in LLVM
summary: MIT/Lawrence Berkeley LLVM-IR-level automatic differentiation: takes LLVM IR for a function and produces LLVM IR for its derivative. Operates after compiler optimisation, so derivatives benefit from optimised forward code. Cross-language (C/C++/Rust/Julia/Fortran/Python) and CPU/GPU.

sections.title: Overview
sections.content:
Enzyme (William Moses et al.) performs automatic differentiation directly on LLVM IR. It takes the IR for a function and emits IR for its derivative — applying AD as an LLVM pass rather than at the source-language level. The pivotal architectural choice: AD runs *after* the compiler's standard optimisations, so the derivative is computed on already-optimised IR and benefits from those optimisations.

Language-agnostic by virtue of LLVM IR: Enzyme works on C, C++, Rust, Julia, Fortran, Python (via Numba), and any other LLVM-frontend language. CPU and GPU (via NVPTX) targets supported.

sections.title: Design position
sections.content:
Most production AD systems (PyTorch's autograd, JAX's `grad`, TensorFlow GradientTape) implement AD at the source-language or framework level: they intercept array operations, build a computation graph, and emit derivative computations from the graph. This works well for tensor-shaped computations but degrades for general code (loops, branches, recursive data structures).

Enzyme's IR-level approach handles general code uniformly: any LLVM-IR-expressible computation can be differentiated. The trade-off is more compiler-engineering overhead and worse error messages — the derivative is shaped by LLVM IR, not by user-friendly source constructs. For mixed scientific-computing workloads where AD needs to work on the same code the user already optimises with LLVM, Enzyme is the natural choice.
